verdicts.note: Repeated explicit claims across S1 ('championed the millionaires and capital gains taxes', cut the sales tax) and S4 (millionaires' tax, progressive reform); +2 high is airtight.

Borderline: JLARC chairmanship (S3) and corporate tax-preference accountability (S4) are real pole_a evidence against a strong invest-and-expand education record, so 0 mixed holds. Minor citation sloppiness: the 'education funding expansion' half of the basis is best sourced to S2 ('leading champion for education funding'), though S4's fund-education language partially covers it.
S2 supports both poles verbatim: removing density barriers and TOD (pole_a supply) alongside required affordable units in new developments (pole_b regulation). Genuinely mixed.

All cited evidence is general environmentalism, not climate/energy policy: Hanford nuclear-waste cleanup [S1], a self-reported WCA endorsement in his pamphlet list [S1], and microplastics washing-machine filters [S4]. Nothing addresses the axis tension (carbon pricing/CCA vs. energy affordability). Davis received 'low' for comparably indirect evidence; consistency requires low here too. Direction +1 is fine.

Seven-term incumbent, JLARC chair, Appropriations [S3][S6] — the maximal proven-record profile; -2 high is exact. Note: the related highlight bullet ('chairs JLARC and sits on Appropriations... [S2]') sources committee assignments to S2 when they come from S3, and the 'fifteen years' from S1/S7.

Checked whether transparency work really maps to the money-and-power axis: WCOG board service, Sunshine honoree, public-records fights, and conflict-of-interest standards for legislators [S2][S4] are a sustained structural-reform record, and the moderate +1 (not +2) correctly reflects that he simultaneously works within establishment/labor donor structures [S6][S7].

Single-source but exact: claiming to have passed 'ICE mask bans' and protected immigrants [S1] matches the rubric's pole_b description ('constrain ICE (masking bans...)') word for word; a claimed enacted record justifies +2, and medium correctly reflects the single source.
Repeated across S1 (funded abortion access) and S2 (reproductive freedom, LGBTQ+ rights, hate-crime laws); +2 high is supported.
Tax wealthy individuals and corporations [S1][S2] plus defending the Seattle Social Housing millionaire tax against Amazon [S1]; repeated and explicit, +2 high stands.

Considered whether this should be +2: free childcare for all, transit investment, universal healthcare with no audit counterweight [S1][S2] is heavily pole_b, but the evidence is platform breadth rather than a maximal spend-first record, so +1 high is a defensible conservative call.
Genuinely mixed with strong evidence on both poles: build-enough-homes abundance urbanism [S2] and social-housing defense plus public-housing rent freezes [S1][S2]. 0 high is right.
Honest low: transit priority and Futurewise environmental-justice board work [S1][S2] are indirect, and the basis says so.
Never held elected office [S1], but the 2023 near-miss council race and coalition/legislative-advocacy record [S1][S4] correctly pull him to +1 rather than Dreher's +2.

The only S4 evidence is that Davis attacks Dreher's AI-industry and out-of-state funding — an oppositional campaign tactic, not a stated money-in-politics platform (no PAC refusal, no structural-reform planks in the dossier; he accepts IBEW and org money [S1][S2]). The 'organized grassroots corporate-taxation campaigns' half of the basis is from S1, not the cited S4, and speaks to taxes more than reform. Inferring a reform-axis position from an attack on a rival is indirect inference → low.
'Universal healthcare' as a funded public good [S1][S2] supports pole_b direction; +1 (not +2, mechanism unspecified) at medium is correctly calibrated.
AI consumer/worker protections added to the platform after Dreher entered [S4][S5]; +1 medium correctly places him between Pollet's silence and Dreher's centerpiece.

'Fix Washington's regressive tax structure' [S1] supports pole_b direction; +1 medium correctly stops short of his rivals' structural specifics, matching the contest file's note that all three favor progressive revenue.
'Cut red tape and fund affordable housing' [S1] genuinely points both ways from a brief pamphlet statement; 0 low is honest.

Borderline but defensible: prosecutor career, 'hold corporations and criminals accountable' [S1][S2], and the Walton-Anderson (former Harrell public-safety chief) endorsement [S2] support a mild pole_a lean. Counter-evidence — 'evidence-based public safety' phrasing and his prosecutions of civil-rights violations BY officers [S1] — is exactly why -1 medium, not -2 high, is right. Also note the endorsement highlight bullet cites [S6] for Evans/Foster, whose actual sources are S1/S2 (S6 covers only The Stranger).
First-time candidate, no legislative or elected experience, entered April 2026 [S1][S4]; maximal outsider profile, +2 high stands.

Signature issue with repeated explicit statements across S1, S2, and S4 ('aggressively regulate tech', AI disinformation, data centers, social-media safeguards); +2 high is airtight. The ~$100K in AI-industry funding [S4] questions independence but does not change his stated platform, and the scoring file surfaces it in highlights.

missing.basis: His pamphlet explicitly pairs both poles: 'hold government accountable for tax dollars' (audit/restrain) alongside fully funding schools and funding affordable housing, childcare, and transit (invest/expand). The scoring file's own highlight quotes this language yet no spending score was assigned; comparable mixed evidence earned Pollet a 0/medium.
